title: What part of an AI trading workflow do you trust the least right now: idea generation, backtesting, execution, or monitoring?
author: Carter_LW
selftext:
I keep noticing that people use "AI trading" to describe very different things.

Sometimes they mean idea generation. Sometimes they mean code generation. Sometimes they mean signal generation. Sometimes they mean full automation all the way through execution.

The more I look at it, the less I think the hard part is getting AI to produce something interesting. The hard part is figuring out where the weak link is once the workflow leaves the toy stage.

For me, the point where things start feeling shaky is usually the handoff between research and execution. A result can look clean in testing, but the moment you ask whether the logic is inspectable, the assumptions are obvious, and the live behavior can be monitored without guessing, confidence drops fast.

If you are actually working with AI in trading, what part of the workflow do you trust the least right now and why?

comments.author: BottleInevitable7278
comments.body: Good ideas generating is the hardest part.

replies.author: Carter_LW
replies.body:
I get that.

A weak idea can still sound smart for way too long, especially once AI helps dress it up. A lot of the damage happens before the idea gets challenged hard enough.

comments.author: Lonely_Corgi_728
comments.body: The Risk On Risk off part.

replies.author: Carter_LW
replies.body:
Yeah, that one gets messy fast.

Risk-on / risk-off sounds simple until you actually have to define what flips it and what confirms it. It can look clean in research and still be the shakiest part once it's live.

What do you usually anchor it to when you think about it that way?

comments.author: melbkiwi
comments.body: AI in trading probably doesn’t fail first because it has no ideas. It fails because people don’t challenge its ideas hard enough before treating them as executable.

replies.author: Carter_LW
replies.body:
Yeah, I think that's the real problem.

It's not that AI can't produce ideas. It's that people start treating the output like executable logic before it has been pushed on hard enough.

replies.author: Carter_LW
replies.body: Exactly. AI can generate plausible-looking ideas all day. The failure usually starts when nobody forces those ideas through hard assumptions, edge cases, execution constraints, and monitoring before calling them tradable.
